coredata.dc:title: Green synthetic strategy of BCNO nanostructure and phosphor-based light – Emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Yue
Yuan, Bo
Zhang, Dongjiu
Ma, Tian
Huang, Xiancong
Chu, Zengyong
Lai, Kan
coredata.dc:description: BCNO phosphor has been paid much attention due to their unique physical, electronic and optical properties. Here we have successfully obtained BCNO nano-particle phosphor by microwave treating from boric acid, urea, and glucose at low temperatures and in short reaction time. Glucose decomposed into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which facilitated the formation of hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). Through our method, GQDs domains were uniformly incorporated into h-BN, leading to the formation of BCNO and decrease of bandgap. BCNO demonstrated excellent performance in light emitting diodes (LEDs) with green and blue light. We envision that this BCNO phosphor will enable the next generation blue and green LED devices due to the easiness of large scale fabrication at an economic cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BCNO
Graphene oxide quantum dots
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The PL mechanism of BCNO phosphor
Fabrication and characterization of BCNO phosphor based blue and green LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of BCNO phosphor and LEDs.

(a–c) SEM images of BCNO-G1.5 phosphor. (d) EDS spectrum of BCNO-G1.5 nano-phosphor, it shows the marked area by orange arrow in (c). (e–f) SEM images of BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor. Inset shows EDS spectrum of BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, it shows the orange marked point in (e). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a and b)TEM images of BCNO-G1.5 phosphor. (c and d) HR-TEM images of the BCNO-G1.5 phosphor. The insert in (d) is the corresponding SEAD of the GQDs region. (e–g) TEM images of BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor. (h) Particle size distribution of BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor material.

(a) and (b) XRD of BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, inset shows the deconvoluted spectra of BCNO-G1.5, which clearly demonstrate the presence of h-BN, B2O3 and nanostructure of graphitic lattices. (c) and (d) FT-IR spectrum of BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor in range of 500–4000cm−1, inset shows the FT-IR spectrum of BCNO samples in range of 1300–1500cm−1.

The XPS spectra of BCNO samples. (a–c) Deconvolved curves of BCNO-G1.5 and (d–f) BCNO-GQD1.0 shown in solid lines and dashed lines respectively. The curves are deconvolved by Gaussian fitting, thereby indicating possible bonding structures.
Plot of ε1/2/λ versus 1/λ for (a) BCNO-G1.5 and (b) BCNO-GQD1.0. Parameters are derived from the UV/vis absorption spectra in the insets.

(a and b) PL decay curve of the BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, respectively. The emission images under (c) and (e) naked eye and (d) and (f) 365nm excitation for the BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, respectively. (f) CIE diagram of BCNO blue phosphor. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

3D-fluorescence spectroscopies of (a and b) BCNO–G1.5 and (e and f) BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, respectively. 2D-fluorescence spectroscopies of (c) BCNO-G1.5 and (g) BCNO-GQD1.0 phosphor, respectively. Emission and excitation spectra of (d) BCNO-G1.5 and (h) BCNO-GQD1.0, respectively.

(a) Blue and green EL emission spectrum of a fabricated phosphor coating on the surface of ultraviolet LEDs. Inset shows the emission images under naked eye excitation for the green and blue LEDs. (b) CIE color co-ordinates of the green and blue LEDs, respectively. (c) the Nano-structure of the UV-LEDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The atomic ratios of BCNO phosphor.
The parameters of green and blue LEDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthetic strategy of BCNO nanostructure and phosphor-based light – Emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Quartermaster Research Institute of General Logistics Department, Beijing 100010, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Quartermaster Research Institute of General Logistics Department

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Defense Institute of China, Beijing 100010, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Space Launching Site Reliability Technology, Hainan 570100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Space Launching Site Reliability Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha 410073, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, National University of Defense Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: BCNO phosphor has been paid much attention due to their unique physical, electronic and optical properties. Here we have successfully obtained BCNO nano-particle phosphor by microwave treating from boric acid, urea, and glucose at low temperatures and in short reaction time. Glucose decomposed into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which facilitated the formation of hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). Through our method, GQDs domains were uniformly incorporated into h-BN, leading to the formation of BCNO and decrease of bandgap. BCNO demonstrated excellent performance in light emitting diodes (LEDs) with green and blue light. We envision that this BCNO phosphor will enable the next generation blue and green LED devices due to the easiness of large scale fabrication at an economic cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of BCNO in 2008 by Ogi et al [1], it has received great attention due to its unique physical, electronic and optical properties [2]. Thus, it has been considered as one of the most promising candidates for future light emitting diodes(LEDs) [3]. For example, T. Ogi reported [4] a facile synthesis method of an RE-free white light emitting phosphor containing Al, B, C, N, and O. Upon excitation at 365nm, it exhibited a broad emission band centered and spanning the entire visible spectrum. In order to tune the emission spectra of BCNO in visible light, nano-carbon doped hexagonal boron nitride was investigated by many groups, based on the different contents of B/C/N/O [1,5]. Ogi et al [1]. prepared BCNO phosphor with different colored fluorescence by reacting the polyethylene glycol(PEG) of different molecular weight, boric acid and urea through a one-step oxidation process. Shuo Yan and co-workers [5] discovered a method using boric acid, melamine and different contents of citric acid to synthesize the multi-colored phosphor from dark-blue to green and bright-yellow. But the photoluminescence(PL) mechanism of BCNO phosphor was not clear, owing to the complex reaction and diversified production. Consequently, many researches [2,6,7] speculated that the origin of the PL is related to defects or the closed-shell BO− and BO2 − anions that act as high-efficiency luminescence centers. Defects can be considered as a main factor to affect the optical properties [7], which consists of the structure change, atoms replacing and nano-carbon doping. It has been reported that the carbon and oxygen defects are crucially important for the defects in the BCNO samples [5].

Carbon nanodots, one of the most important constitutes of nano-carbon, gained tremendous popularity because of their potential in cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, high stability and excellent photoluminescence [8–13]. Generally, bottom-up method is currently preferred in the synthesis of carbon nanodots, which involves thermal oxidation [8], microwave irradiation [9], hydrothermal synthesis [12,13] and so on. F.A. Permatasari [13] synthesized N-doped GQDs by reacting citric acid and urea via a hydrothermal method, which exhibited a color – changed performance. Sungan Do et al. [14] also synthesized N-doped carbon nano-dots through soft-template using citric acid and ethylenediamine(EDA), which could obtain products but without safety concerns. Moreover, these processes required high synthesis temperature and wasted a lot of energy. Therefore, an economic method is strongly imperative. Recently, Tang et al [9]. used glucose containing water-solution to synthesize carbon nanodots by a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal route at low temperature and in short reaction time. However, the synthetic method is simple but use inconvenient, because the water-solubility of carbon-nanodots hampered its application in electronic and optoelectronic devices vulnerable to water [14,15].

Semiconductor carbon nano-dots, known as graphene quantum dots(GQDs), which has more fascinating merits than other carbon nanodots, such as stable photoluminescence, high specific surface area, high mobility and tunable band gap [10]. Theoretical and experimental studies [14] have shown that GQDs (less than 10nm in size) possess a good pronounced edge effect and stronger quantum confinement effect, resulting in bandgap of GQDs-based phosphor easy tuned through doping, changing the size and shape of GQDs, manipulating GQDs through partial reduction and surface passivation.

In this paper, we investigated microwave heating for synthesis of BCNO phosphor with boric acid, urea and glucose as the raw materials [16–21]. Glucose decomposed into GQDs, which are doped into h-BN lattices and resulted in the change of bandgap. At the same time, we set up a controlled experiment to realize the PL mechanism of the obtained BCNO phosphor. Furthermore, the synthesized BCNO phosphor was dispersed in Si-resin with curing agent for the blue and green LEDs.

BCNO phosphor were synthesized from boric acid(B(OH)3), urea((NH2)2CO) and glucose(C6H12O6) (or GQDs), which were used as boron, nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. Boric acid (99.5%) was purchased from Taishan Shiji Co. Ltd. GQDs was purchased from XFNANO Co. Ltd. Glucose (99.0%) and Urea (99.0%) were obtained from Beijing chemical work. All chemicals were used without further purification.

The detail synthesis method is described as follows ( Fig. 1) ( Table 1): Precursor solution was prepared by mixing boric acid, urea and glucose in deionized water followed by stirring until they were dissolved. The porcelain cup was then placed in a commercial single function microwave oven (Galanz, 800W, G80F20CN2L), where it was heated by microwaving with an oven output of 800W for 10min to obtain the phosphor. BCNO-GQD1.0 was synthesized by changing the carbon source materials in the same atmosphere. GQD represents for the different carbon-source materials, which is purified graphene oxide quantum dots (Fig. 1).

For the fabrication of LEDs, the synthesized BCNO-G1.5 phosphor of 6mg was dispersed in Si-resin solution and then vigorously stirred(mixture of resin and a curing agent which acts as a hardener). A mixture was used for coating the surface of ultraviolet LEDs (The parameters of LEDs: 370mW power, 60° reflector angle, DC 3.5V, model UV 4.5mm×4.5mm 1-island type, made in Korea). Afterwards, it was dried at 200°C for few hours before packaging (Table 1).

The surface morphologies of the BCNO hybrid nanostructures were characterized by SEM using JSM-6700F microscope. The crystalline structure was investigated by X-ray diffraction(XRD) on a D8ADVANCE type, using Cu Ka radiation with 2θ from 10° to 90°. The fourier transform infrared(FT-IR) spectra of the samples were obtained using the KBr pellet method by Nicolte magna-IR 760 spectrometer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded at room temperature with a UV-1800 spectrophotometer. TEM was conducted using a JEM-2100F electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. XPS was performed using K Alpha 1063 type with focused monochromatized Al Ka radiation (1486.6eV), to determine changes in the atomic ratios and the existence of functional groups. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was performed using the associated equipments of SEM. The PL spectra of the BCNO phosphor were recorded using a spectrofluorophotometer (FL3, France) equipped with a xenon laser source. The 3-D spectra (Emission–Excitation-Matrices or EEMs) were obtained using excitation wavelengths from 250 to 400nm and emission wavelengths from 400 to 700nm with intervals of 10nm. The EQE of the prepared particles was determined at an excitation wavelength of 365nm produced by a 150W Xe lamp using an absolute PL quantum yield measurement system (Quantaurus-QY, Japan) with an A-10095-01 powder sample holder. The PL decay profiles and the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) were collected with an FL3 spectrophotometer.
To explore the surface morphology and microstructural of the BCNO phosphor, we performed SEM and TEM measurements.

Typical SEM images of BCNO-G1.5 are shown in Fig. 2(a–d). It reveals some small clumpy materials combined with the 2D-oriented nanostructure formed a layer-by-layer structure at micrometer scale. Fig. 2(e and f) show BCNO-GQD1.0 particles are irregularly shaped and closely attached. From these images, we can see that the size of obtained particles is much bigger than the other group reported [3,22]. A Possible explanation for our obtained BCNO phosphor is the long time reaction. The phosphor elemental analysis with energy dispersive spectroscopy(EDS) confirms the presence of B, C, N and O in two samples.

Fig. 3(a–d) shows typical TEM images of BCNO-G1.5. The low-magnification images in Fig. 3(a and b) indicate the presence of nanosheets similar to few-layered h-BN. HR-TEM images and selected-area electron diffraction patterns taken from several areas show more details of BCNO (Fig. 3(c and d)). They show clear lattice structure with lattice parameters of 0.21nm corresponding to the (1100) lattice fringes of sp2-type graphene [8,23], which means typical GQDs. These GQDs dispersed well and with a diameter distribution of 2.1–2.7nm, which are down to 10nm [24–26]. Thus, it is concluded that microwave heating can produce the nano-sized GQDs-well-dispersed BCNO phosphor and the layered nanosheets have non-crystalline structure, which also supported by the followed XRD results. As shown in Fig. 3(e–h), GQDs also existed in BCNO-GQD1.0 and its size was concentrated in about 7.5nm.

In conclusion, smaller GQDs could also be gained in reaction system of boric acid, urea and glucose, which were smaller in size and dispersed well in BCNO samples. The GQDs in reaction system may facilitate formation and agglomeration of BCNO, resulting in size increase of BCNO particles.

As shown in the inset picture of Fig. 4(a), the peaks in XRD pattern are deconvolved into three ones by Gaussian fitting, resulting that there are five peaks, 15.4°, 26.2°, 27.8°, 28.4° and 42.8°. XRD pattern of BCNO-G1.5 reveals the existence of B2O3, BN and graphitic carbon lattices(or GQDs). The three peaks centered at 15.4°, 27.8° and 42.8°, which signifies the formation of cubic B2O3. Additionally, there is a typical distinct peak at 28.4° corresponding to the graphitic carbon lattices(or GQDs) [27], which appears together with BN and B2O3 peaks. The overlapped signals at 26.2° and 42.8° indicate the presence of h-BN [28]. In order to examine the BCNO-GQD1.0 phase purity, we also performed the XRD (Fig. 4(b)) of it. The result is almost as same as BCNO-G1.5.

A typical FT-IR spectrum of BCNO-GQD1.0 in the range of 500–4000cm−1 is shown in Fig. 4(c). The strong absorption bands centered at 925.97cm−1 and 1384.08cm−1 unambiguously denote the presence of B–N, which can be attributed to the in-plane and out-of–plane vibration modes of the sp2 hybridized BN [29,30].The bands shifted can be ascribed to the incorporation of GQDs into the h-BN lattices. The vibrational bands peaking at ~1253.28cm−1 and 696.55cm−1 attributed to the B–O bands. A distinct band is observed at ~1103.89cm−1 related to the B–C stretching bond. Besides, the two sharp edges are observed at ~1437.81cm−1 and 1359.68cm−1 correspond to –CH and O–N=O bonds. The obviously peaks in the neighborhood of 1664.56cm−1, 1401.03cm−1 and 1026.40cm−1, indicating the presence of –OH, C–O and C=O bonds, respectively. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 4(d)) shows the B–N(1384.08cm−1) band of BCNO-GQD1.0 becomes increasingly clear in BCNO-G1.5(1384.22cm−1) and the signal assigned to N–O appears obviously at the same position [31–35].
The above analysis means the nanostructure of carbon lattices is present in the hybrid particles and glucose decomposition facilitated formation of hexagonal boron nitride(h-BN).

A deep study on the bonding structure of the BCNO-G1.5 phosphor was made using XPS C1s, B1s and N1s profiles, as shown in Fig. 5(a–c). According to B1s analysis (Fig. 5(a)), the single peak at 192.58eV is ascribed to B2O3 and B–O bonds [33,34]. The C1s in Fig. 5(b) can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 284.78eV, 286.08eV and 288.98eV [32]. The C1s peak is composed mostly of C–C(284.78eV) bonds components with additional broadening a possible peak due to forming of GQDs and a possible contribution from O–C–OH(288.98eV). Moreover, the lower binding energy was apparent, which possibly indicates formation of C–OH(286.08eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5(c)) of the BCNO-G1.5 also shows three peaks at 399.98eV, 401.48eV and 402.48eV, which are attributed to the B–N–C, graphitic N and N–C=O(N–O) bonds, respectively.

Compared to the BCNO-G1.5, the strong bonds graphitic N(402.48eV) of BCNO-GQD1.0 becomes disappeared (Fig. 5(f)) and a signal assigned to C–OH(286.08eV) distinctly appears (Fig. 5e), suggesting the existence of oxygen-containing groups in the GQDs as well as the transformation of N doped GQDs. So during the microwave treatment, possible reactions include,
(1) CO(NH2)2(s)+H2O→CO2+2NH3
(2) B–O(s)+NH3→B–N(s)+H2O
(3) C–O(s)+NH3→C–N(s)+H2O

The elemental changes were obtained from XPS analysis and are listed in Table 2. We can see that the formulas for BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 are B1.0C64.1N8.6O45.4 and B1.0C63.1N22.3O60.6, respectively. Because Eq. (2) is reacted in two samples, we can make an assumption that B remains stable during the microwave treatment. Thus, it can be concluded that O and N increased during the microwave treatment in BCNO-GQDs1.0, resulting in the formation of N-doped GQDs.

Fig. 6 shows the BCNO samples have an absorption spectrum mostly in the ultraviolet region [18,36]. In spectrum, BCNO-GQD1.0 and BCNO-G1.5 had absorption peaks at 274nm, 334nm and 313nm, respectively. The origin of these peaks is related to π electron transition in nitrogen and oxygen-containing GQD, these absorption were related to different electron transition in BCNO. The absorption peak at 334nm and 313nm is due to electron transitions in N/B-doped GQDs domains. The absorption at 274nm corresponds to electron transition in h-BN domains conjugated with GQDs domains [13,33].

The UV/vis absorption spectrum was carried out to investigate the band gap of the BCNO samples, based on their optically induced transition. As shown in Fig. 6, BCNO-G1.5 has two band gaps, 3.1 and 4.1eV, BCNO-GQD1.0 has three band gaps, 2.8, 3.9 and 5.1eV. 3.1eV of BCNO-G1.5 and 2.8eV of BCNO-GQD1.5 are related to the isolated N-doped GQDs in two samples. 3.1eV, bigger than 2.8ev, is resulted by smaller GQDs and stronger quantum confinement effect. Other band gaps are related to the h-BN domains and the doped GQDs make these band gaps smaller than these reported by other groups. For BCNO-GQD1.0, GQDs couldn׳t disperse well in raw materials. So, the doped GQDs are uniformly distributed in h-BN domains of BCNO-GQD1.0, which result in two band gaps, 3.9 and 5.1eV. Compared to BCNO-GQD1.0, GQDs and h-BN domains in BCNO-G1.5 has only one band gap, 4.1eV. It indicates the doped GQDs dispersed well, which is benefit by uniform raw materials.

The PL decay was recorded for the emission at 365nm excitation wavelength. For the BCNO samples, the decay time are in the range of nanoseconds. Since excitation lifetime is a size – dependent parameter, the smaller the size of the phosphor, the shorter the excitation life and the recombination rate will be.

The lifetime data of the BCNO phosphor transitions are very well fitted to a double-exponential function as described by the equation [22]:
I
(
t
)
=
A
1
exp
(
−
t
/
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
(
−
t
/
τ
2
)
where τ 1 and τ 2 are the decay lifetimes of the luminescence, and A 1 and A 2 are the weighting parameters.

The lifetimes for the BCNO-G1.5 ( Fig. 7(a)) and BCNO-GQD1.0 (Fig. 7(b)) phosphor are τ 1 ~1.883ns, τ 2 ~7.531ns and τ 1 ~5426ns, τ 2 ~2.170ns, respectively. For BCNO samples, there are many defects such as N vacancy, substitutional defects and B/O-related defects near crystalline sites in the amorphous parts. these defects would generate different defect levels and affect the fluorescent lifetime. The change of lifetimes τ 1 and τ 2 might be related to the carbon and oxygen impurity concentration during GQDs reacted with h-BN when glucose decomposition [18,22]. The CIE color co-ordinates obtained from emission spectra are X=0.1726, y=0.1951 and X=0.1495, y=0.1696 for BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0, as shown in Fig. 7(g). They are located typically in the blue region. Fig. 7(c–f) represents the synthesized BCNO phosphor powder, left pictures exhibit the powder under room light with white body color, while the right digital photographs indicate blue emission appeared under UV lamp irradiation at 365nm. It is revealed that under direct observation by naked eye, the phosphor׳ color changed from blue to dark blue. The fluorescence coloring was uniform throughout the sample without color mottling.

To further explore the optical properties of the as-prepared BCNO samples, we carried out a 3D- and 2D- detailed fluorescence spectroscopy study ( Fig. 8(a–c) and (e–g)). As shown in Fig. 8(c) and (g), the synthesized BCNO samples fluoresced in the visible region of 400–600nm and the samples exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior, which are shifted from 424nm to 459nm and 411nm to 456nm for BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 respectively, when the excitation wavelength is changed from 320nm to 380nm.

Fig. 8(a and b)(e and f) present the detailed 3D-fluorescence spectroscopy for BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0 with different excitation ranging from 250nm to 400nm [37]. The emission range of BCNO-G1.5 is much broader than the other one. This can be explained from the particle size of the samples. The TEM analysis shown in Fig. 3 found the small pieces of carbon (or GQDs) produced by glucose decomposition are size difference , which is attributable to the different broad emission. Apparently, the broad and excitation-dependents effects emission of BCNO samples are similar to the former one, which reflects effects from both sp2 of different size and different emission sites of each sp2 clusters. Fig. 8(d) and (h) shows emission and excitation spectra of BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0, respectively [38]. In the emission spectrum, the peaks occur at 447nm and 452nm, which represent a shift of 82nm and 85nm from the emission peak at 365nm. The efficiency of the fluorescence is reflected by the high absolute quantum yield(QY) as measured with the comparison method, we observed QY of 27.1% and 30.9% for BCNO-G1.5 and BCNO-GQD1.0, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PL mechanism of BCNO phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we demonstrate that the commonly and widely used glucose can be used as a starting material for growing BCNO phosphor. Glucose decomposed into GO quantum dots with high active oxygen-containing groups played a role of reaction sites with B and N atoms. It could be said that quantum dots were reaction nucleation and facilitated the formation of h-BN.

Replacing oxygen functional groups on the GO sheet edge with nitrogen/boron-containing groups transforms GO into a semiconductor, which is changed the optical and electronic properties. Based on the above results, a modified PL mechanism [6,7] of BCNO phosphor relies on attributed to the core shell BO− and BO2− anions acting as luminescent centers or impurity defects specially nitrogen vacancies in BCNO phosphor. GQDs, doped into h-BN lattices, changed band-gap easily and formed a series of new levels under the nitrogen vacancy level. So, the transition from the conduction band or nitrogen vacancy level to the carbon related levels will emit blue visible color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of BCNO phosphor based blue and green LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(a) presents the EL spectrum of commercial ultraviolet LEDs coated with as synthesized mixture phosphor. It reveals the PL peak positions of blue and green light at 365nm are 492.47nm and 448.72nm respectively, and it exhibits a narrow emissions. The CIE color coordinates of green and blue LEDs are shown in Fig. 9(b). It is quite evident from this figure that emission of synthesized LEDs lies considerably in the green and blue region with color co-ordinates x=0.3057, y=0.4751 and x=0.2292, y=0.2997, and the correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6163.4 and 16,607, respectively. Thus, the obtained excellent LEDs produce green and blue light, indicating that it can be readily used in the special light LEDs in the next generation ( Table 3).

In this study, we have successfully obtained the BCNO phosphor by microwaving boric acid, urea, and glucose at low temperature and in short reaction time. Glucose decomposed into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which facilitated the formation of hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). Through our method, GQDs domains were uniformly incorporated into h-BN, leading to the formation of BCNO and decrease of bandgap. BCNO-G1.5 exhibits a highly intensity blue emission peak centered at 447nm(using a 365nm excitation wavelength) with quantum efficiency of about 27.1%. The obtained LEDs with BCNO-G1.5 emit green and blue light with excellent performance. Finally, we envision that this BCNO phosphor will enable the next generation blue and green LED devices, because of the easy obtained in a large scale at an economic cost.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11404397).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.07.057.
